title: 1. The Problem
content: Quantum mechanics is our most successful physical theory. It predicts the behavior of atoms, molecules, and light to ten decimal places. No experiment has ever contradicted it.

title: 2. The Hidden Choice
content: In December 1900, Max Planck presented his derivation of the blackbody radiation law to the German Physical Society in Berlin. His solution to the ultraviolet catastrophe introduced the quantum of action.

title: 3. The Alternative
content: If you adopt Hensel's p-adic metric — distance by divisibility rather than magnitude — the geometry of the state space changes fundamentally. It is no longer a smooth, continuous surface. It is a tree.

title: 4. The Mechanism
content: A concrete example makes the mechanism vivid. Consider four points on a simplified tree, each defined by a sequence of four binary choices (0 or 1, left or right).

title: 5. The Resolution
content: When quantum mechanics is re-examined through the ultrametric lens, each of its foundational puzzles dissolves. The measurement problem, wave-particle duality, the Born rule, decoherence, nonlocality, and the irregularity of primes.

title: 6. The Evidence
content: A scientific proposal must be testable. The ultrametric paradigm makes specific, falsifiable predictions: log-periodic oscillations in the CMB, prime-modulated quantum noise, and threshold behavior in tree-structured gates.

title: 7. The Invitation
content: The Archimedean metric was never chosen. It was inherited. Planck did not decide to use it in 1900; he used it because it was the only geometry he knew. The choice is visible now.

title: References
content: Hensel, K. (1897). Hilbert, D. (1897). Bruhat, F. and Tits, J. (1972). Monna, A. F. (1968). Shapiro, H. N. (1983). Vladimirov, V. S., Volovich, I. V., and Zelenov, E. I. (1994).
